name: Zonules.com Claim Registry
note: Every scientific claim is supported by a registered source, marked as conceptual interpretation, marked as internal framework, or excluded. No unsourced scientific claim may be published.

claims.statement: The zonules of Zinn are fine fibrous strands that suspend the crystalline lens from the ciliary body.
The zonular apparatus, also called the suspensory ligament of the lens, holds the lens in centered geometric tension.
Tension transmitted through the zonules allows the ciliary muscle to change lens shape during accommodation, shifting focus across distances.
The structure is named after Johann Gottfried Zinn, the 18th-century anatomist who described it.
The zonules are the literal instance of the suspension layer (FIO-01): focus is produced by hidden structural tension, not by the lens alone.
The ciliary body is a ring of tissue behind the iris that contains the ciliary muscle and gives rise to the zonular fibers.
Contraction of the ciliary muscle reduces tension on the zonules, allowing the elastic lens to become more convex for near focus.
The ciliary body also produces aqueous humor, the fluid that fills the anterior segment of the eye.
The ciliary body is the force source of the suspension layer (FIO-01): it does not focus light but governs the tension that makes focus adjustable.
The crystalline lens is a transparent, biconvex structure suspended behind the iris that refracts light to help form an image on the retina.
The lens is elastic and changes curvature during accommodation.
The lens is enclosed in an elastic capsule into which the zonular fibers insert.
The lens is the focal element of the suspension system: present but unable to focus reliably without zonular tension.
Accommodation is the process by which the eye changes its optical power to maintain a clear image of objects at varying distances.
During accommodation for near vision, the ciliary muscle contracts, zonular tension decreases, and the lens becomes more spherical.
Accommodative ability typically declines with age, a condition known as presbyopia.
Accommodation is the operational expression of FIS-2: focus integrity maintained across an intended operating range.
The lens capsule is a transparent, elastic basement-membrane envelope that surrounds the crystalline lens.
The zonular fibers insert into the lens capsule around the lens equator, transmitting their tension to the lens through the capsule.
The elasticity of the capsule helps mold the lens into a more curved shape when zonular tension is released during accommodation.
The lens capsule is the transmission interface of the suspension layer: the surface through which hidden tension becomes a change in focal geometry.
Visual perception is the process by which the brain organizes and interprets visual input into a stable, meaningful representation of the environment.
Perception is constructive: the visual system actively organizes input rather than passively recording it, as shown by perceptual organization and constancy.
Selective attention determines which parts of the visual field are processed in detail, holding a focal target stable against competing stimuli.

Perception is the Layer 02 instance of the suspension thesis: attention holds a focal interpretation in place the way the zonules hold the lens, and its collapse is the perceptual form of suspension failure (FIO-01).
Machine vision is the field of methods by which artificial systems detect, classify, localize, and interpret content in images and video.
Machine vision systems can produce correct detections while failing to interpret a scene correctly, distinguishing detection from understanding.
Establishing the origin and authenticity of an image is addressed by content provenance standards rather than by detection alone.

Machine vision is the Layer 03 instance of the focus-integrity thesis: calibration and grounding play the suspension role, and machine failures map onto the FIO classes (signal-noise, provenance, interpretation).
Selective attention is the mechanism by which the visual system prioritizes some stimuli for detailed processing while suppressing others.
Attention can be directed voluntarily (top-down) or captured involuntarily (bottom-up) by salient stimuli.
Attentional capacity is limited, so not all of the visual field can be processed in detail at the same time.
Selective attention is the perceptual suspension mechanism (FIO-01): it holds a focal target in place, and its failure is the perceptual form of suspension collapse.
Figure-ground organization is the perceptual process of segregating a scene into objects (figure) and background (ground).
Figure-ground assignment can be ambiguous, as demonstrated by reversible figures where the same image yields two organizations.
Gestalt principles describe how the visual system groups elements into coherent figures.
Figure-ground separation is the perceptual instance of signal-noise integrity (FIO-03): when it collapses, signal cannot be separated from ground.
Deepfakes are synthetic media in which a person's likeness is generated or manipulated using machine-learning methods.
Detection methods analyze artifacts, inconsistencies, or statistical signatures to classify media as authentic or synthetic.
Detection alone does not establish origin; content provenance approaches record an image's source and edit history.
Deepfake detection is a focus-integrity problem of provenance (FIO-04): the system must establish whether what it sees is real.
Image provenance is the recorded history of where an image came from and how it was modified.
Content provenance standards attach tamper-evident metadata to media to support authenticity verification.
Provenance complements detection by establishing origin rather than inferring manipulation from the pixels alone.
Image provenance is the infrastructure of provenance integrity (FIO-04): it answers the origin question that focus alone cannot.
The cornea is the transparent, avascular anterior surface of the eye and provides approximately two-thirds of the eye's total refractive power.
Corneal transparency depends on the precise arrangement and hydration of collagen fibrils in the stroma, maintained by active metabolic processes.
The cornea is a focus-integrity element of signal quality (FIO-03): its transparency determines whether the optical signal is clear or degraded before it reaches the lens.
The retina is the light-sensitive tissue lining the posterior interior of the eye, containing photoreceptors and multiple layers of neural processing cells.
The retina converts incident light into neural signals through phototransduction, with the neural output carried by the optic nerve toward the brain.
The retina is the anatomical site where focus integrity is decided (FIO-05): the signal is received, but the interpretation it enables depends entirely on where and how it is focused.

Photoreceptors are specialized retinal cells that transduce light into neural signals; rods are sensitive to low light and motion, cones are concentrated at the fovea and detect color and fine detail.
The highest density of cone photoreceptors is at the fovea, the retinal region responsible for the sharpest central vision.
Photoreceptor density and distribution across the retina determine the spatial resolution and luminance sensitivity available to the visual system.
Photoreceptors are the signal-noise boundary of anatomy (FIO-03): below their threshold there is no signal; above it, signal quality depends on receptor density and health.
The optic nerve is the bundle of approximately one million axons that carry retinal ganglion cell output from the eye to the lateral geniculate nucleus and primary visual cortex.
Damage to the optic nerve causes permanent loss of the visual field it carries, because retinal ganglion cell axons in adults do not regenerate.
The optic nerve is the structural conduit of vision (FIO-01): without its physical integrity, no signal from the retina reaches interpretation, regardless of how well the optical system focuses.
Perceptual constancy is the tendency of the perceptual system to maintain a stable representation of an object despite variations in viewing angle, distance, illumination, or surrounding context.
Classical forms of perceptual constancy include size constancy, shape constancy, and lightness constancy, each representing a stable perceived property across changing sensory conditions.
Constancy depends on the visual system's ability to discount the conditions of observation and infer what properties of the world are invariant, a form of perceptual inference.
Perceptual constancy is the perceptual analogue of interpretation integrity (FIS-5): a system with intact constancy holds a stable interpretation of reality across varying conditions.
The Gestalt principles are rules describing how the visual system organizes elements into perceptual wholes; proximity, similarity, continuity, closure, and figure-ground are the core grouping laws.
Gestalt organization reflects fundamental tendencies of the perceptual system to structure visual input into coherent units, not learned from individual experience alone.
Failure of Gestalt organization, as in certain perceptual disorders or ambiguous figures, produces an unstable or fragmented percept that cannot sustain a stable interpretation.
Gestalt principles are the structural grammar of perceptual signal separation (FIO-03): they determine what counts as signal and what counts as noise at the organizational level.
Computer vision is the field concerned with enabling machines to extract meaningful information from digital images and video, including recognition, detection, segmentation, and scene understanding.
Modern computer vision systems use learned representations, with deep convolutional networks having become dominant for most recognition tasks, replacing hand-crafted feature engineering.
Computer vision systems operate on pixel arrays as primary input; the quality of the input signal — resolution, noise, illumination — directly affects the performance of any downstream task.

Computer vision is the machine layer in which all five FIO failure classes can be instantiated: suspension (calibration), accommodation (scale and focus), signal-noise (input quality), provenance (image origin), and interpretation (model output).
Image classification is the task of assigning a categorical label to an image from a predefined set of classes; it is one of the foundational benchmark tasks of computer vision.
Classification performance depends on the model's ability to learn discriminative features that generalize from training examples to previously unseen inputs.
Distribution shift — when test images come from a different distribution than the training set — is a systematic source of classification failure even in high-accuracy systems.

Image classification is a focus-integrity problem of interpretation (FIO-05): the system receives a signal, constructs an internal representation, and must produce the correct categorical interpretation.
